name: Wewe Gombel
description: Wewe Gombel is a female ghost or spirit in Indonesian mythology. She is often depicted as an old woman with sagging breasts, which she uses to kidnap children. The Wewe Gombel is believed to abduct children who are neglected by their parents or who have been abused. Unlike other malevolent spirits, the Wewe Gombel doesn't harm the children she takes; instead, she cares for them and protects them, acting as a surrogate mother. Her motivations stem from her own tragic past, often said to be a woman who was murdered or committed suicide due to mistreatment by her husband. She seeks to provide the love and care she was denied to other vulnerable children. She is often associated with abandoned buildings, large trees, and areas outside of villages, preying on children who wander off alone during dusk or night. She disappears when the parents show genuine remorse and actively seek to improve their parenting. The Wewe Gombel serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of parental responsibility and the consequences of child neglect.
subtitle: The protector of neglected children.
appearance: Wewe Gombel is typically described as an old woman with long, disheveled hair and large, sagging breasts that are often exposed. Her skin is pale and wrinkled, and her eyes are red or glowing. She is often depicted wearing tattered clothing and has a haunting presence.
habitat: Abandoned buildings, large trees, graveyards, and areas on the outskirts of villages, especially during dusk and night.
behavior: The Wewe Gombel kidnaps children who are neglected, abused, or wander off alone. She doesn't harm them but instead cares for them and protects them as a surrogate mother. She disappears when the parents show genuine remorse and demonstrate a commitment to better parenting.

keyMyths.title: The Neglected Child
keyMyths.description: A child wanders off alone after being neglected by their parents and is taken by the Wewe Gombel. The parents, consumed by guilt, search for their child and promise to be better parents, leading to the child's return.

sources.author: Various Indonesian Folklore Tellers
sources.description: Stories passed down through generations about the Wewe Gombel.
sources.text: Stories passed down through generations about the Wewe Gombel.
